5: A fulfillment: God glorified Christ by saying "You are my Son; today I have begotten you" (Psalms 2:7) — the divine appointment of the Son to high-priestly office fulfills what the royal-sonship psalm anticipated. The crucified-and-risen Jesus is recognized by God as the Son at the moment of his exaltation; the Psalm's "today" is the day of the resurrection-exaltation, the inaugural day of the new priestly order.
6: A fulfillment: "You are a priest forever, according to the order of Melchizedek" (Psalms 110:4) — the priest-forever promised by divine oath is fulfilled in Jesus whose indestructible resurrection life grounds an eternal priesthood. The Psalm's promise of a non-Levitical, oath-backed, eternal priesthood points forward to the one whose death and resurrection constitute the qualitatively different basis of priestly access. Melchizedek was the shadow; Christ is the substance — the king of righteousness and peace who is priest forever by divine oath.
8: A direct revelation: although he was a Son, he learned obedience through what he suffered, and being made perfect he became the source of eternal salvation. The incarnate Son's pathway of suffering-to-completion is not the story of a merely human figure becoming more than he was, but the revelation of how the divine Son accomplished from within human experience what redemption required. The obedience he learned is the obedience he now is — having walked the path of faithful submission to the Father through suffering, he is the source of the same salvation for all who obey him.
10: A fulfillment: designated by God as high priest according to the order of Melchizedek — the Psalms 110:4 oracle's fulfillment frames the entire high-priestly Christology that Hebrews 5:1-10 introduces and Hebrews 7-10 elaborate. The Melchizedekian designation is not a new institution but the fulfillment of what the Genesis 14 figure and the Psalm 110 promise had held out as the type and anticipation of the definitive high priest.
1: A type: every high priest is taken from among humans and appointed to represent them before God. The Aaronic high priesthood is the institutional type whose structure — chosen from humans, representing humans, offering for sins — is fulfilled and exceeded in Christ. The type establishes what a genuine high priest must be (human, appointed, representative, sacrificing) so that when Christ meets all these criteria and exceeds them in each dimension, the argument for his superior priesthood is grounded in the type's own logic.
2: A type: the high priest's ability to deal gently with the ignorant and wayward, because he himself is subject to weakness, is the type of the sympathy Christ shows (Hebrews 4:15). The Levitical high priest sympathizes because he is weak; Christ sympathizes because he was tested. The type points to the genuine human solidarity of the high priest as essential to his function; the antitype fulfills this through the incarnation's actual entry into human weakness without itself becoming sinful.
3: A type: the high priest must offer for his own sins as well as the people's — this structural feature of the Levitical system points forward by contrast to the one who had no sins of his own to offer for. The self-offering requirement reveals both the Levitical high priest's limitation (he is himself a sinner) and Christ's surpassing qualification (he offered himself without sin, as the spotless sacrifice). The type is fulfilled by contrast: Christ does what the Levitical priest could not — offering himself as the untainted victim.
4: A type: no one takes the priestly honor on himself, but only one who is called by God. The divine-call requirement establishes the principle of legitimate appointment that Christ will fulfill through Psalms 110:4's oath. Aaron was called; Christ was called — but Christ's call comes with an oath that Aaron's did not, and from an order (Melchizedek's) that surpasses Aaron's. The shared principle (divine call required) links the type and antitype while the differences in the call's quality distinguish them.
7: A direct revelation: in the days of his flesh, the Son offered prayers and supplications with loud cries and tears, and was heard because of his reverence. The Gethsemane prayer is not a crisis of the incarnation but its most profound expression: the Son in the full reality of human anguish, bringing human need before the Father with complete trust. He was heard — not by removal of death but by resurrection through death. This reveals the character of the Son's high-priestly intercession: real petition, real anguish, real divine hearing.
9: A direct revelation: made perfect, he became the source of eternal salvation for all who obey him. The completed perfection-through-suffering is the qualification for the office whose benefit is eternal. The "eternal" salvation (aiōnios sōtēria) stands in contrast to the annual Yom Kippur atonement: what the Day of Atonement provided temporarily and cyclically, Christ provides permanently and finally. He is not one more source of atonement in a series but the singular source of the salvation that the entire atonement series was pointing toward.
11: A theme: there is much to say about Melchizedek but it is hard to explain because of spiritual dullness. The pastoral interruption is itself Christological: the community's inability to receive advanced Christological teaching (the Melchizedek priesthood) reflects their failure to grow into the fullness of what Christ is and has done. The depth of Christology corresponds to the depth of spiritual maturity — advancing in the faith means advancing in understanding the Son's priestly identity and work.
12: A theme: you need to be taught the elementary principles again rather than being teachers yourselves. The community's theological regression — needing to go back to basics — is a regression in Christological understanding. The "solid food" they are not ready for (v14) is precisely the Melchizedek theology of Hebrews 7-10: the full account of Christ's eternal high priesthood. Their spiritual immaturity is their inability to receive and hold the full Christological claim Hebrews is building toward.
13: A theme: whoever lives on milk is an infant, unskilled in the word of righteousness. The "word of righteousness" in context is the Christological word — the advanced teaching about Christ's priesthood and its achievement of the righteousness that the Levitical system could only symbolize. Spiritual infancy is Christological infancy: not knowing how to handle the teaching about the Son's identity and work with the maturity it requires.
14: A theme: solid food is for the mature who have trained their perceptual faculties to distinguish good from evil. The trained perception required for mature Christian faith is the ability to discern the depths of Christological truth — to see how Christ fulfills, surpasses, and replaces the Levitical system; to trace the typological connections; to hold the "already" and "not yet" in the appropriate tension. This is the theological formation that Hebrews is itself attempting to provide for the community.
